Maladie à Virus Ebola — Situation Report

Democratic Republic of Congo

Published

22 May 2026

Introduction

The Democratic Republic of Congo is experiencing an outbreak of Maladie à Virus Ebola (MVE) with cases reported across health zones in Ituri and Nord-Kivu provinces. This report summarises the epidemiological situation based on data extracted from official INSP DRC situation reports. It is generated automatically and reflects the most recent available data.

Methods

Case count data were extracted from PDF situation reports published by the Institut National de Santé Publique (INSP) DRC using an automated extraction pipeline. Suspect and probable cases are combined into a single unconfirmed category, as individual situation reports differ in which field they populate. Cumulative counts are enforced to be monotonically non-decreasing using the cumulative maximum across successive reporting dates.

Results

As of 20 May 2026, the DRC MVE outbreak had recorded a cumulative total of 732 cases (61 confirmed, 671 suspect or probable) and 160 deaths, corresponding to a case fatality ratio of 21.9%. Cases have been reported across 8 health zones in 2 provinces.

Current counts by health zone

Table 1 presents the most recent cumulative case counts disaggregated by health zone, drawn from the latest available situation report for each zone.

Cross-source validation

Cumulative zone-level counts from this pipeline are compared against the Kraemer lab Ebola DRC 2026 repository, which independently processes the same INSP SitRep PDFs into a standardised long-format. Zone names differ between sources at two sites (Mongbwalu/Mongbalu and Nyankunde/Nyakunde); counts are matched on the harmonised name. ND values in the Kraemer lab series (metric not reported on that date) are treated as missing.

Table 2 presents numerical differences at the most recent date available in each source for each zone.

Discussion

The outbreak trajectory shows a consistent increase in suspect and probable case counts across the reporting period. Confirmed cases remain a minority of the total, reflecting the challenges of laboratory confirmation in the field setting. The geographic spread across both Ituri and Nord-Kivu provinces underscores the need for multi-province coordination of the response.

Per-zone new case data are only available from 20 May 2026 onwards, as earlier situation reports did not disaggregate new cases by health zone. Results should be interpreted in the context of reporting delays and the possibility of incomplete situation report coverage for some health zones and dates. The case fatality ratio reported here should be treated as a provisional estimate, as delays in death reporting may affect the numerator. Where the two independently extracted series diverge in the cross-source comparison, differences likely reflect the timing of PDF processing, minor OCR discrepancies, or differences in zone-date attribution between pipelines. Close agreement between the two extractions provides supporting evidence for the reliability of the counts reported here.
